straight_beat.name: Lenard Chouteau
straight_beat.column:
The Cardinals lost again. Not in a way that’s shocking, but in a way that feels inevitable, like a slow drip of water wearing down a stone. Boston’s 9-3 romp wasn’t just about the Red Sox’ offense—it was about St. Louis’ inability to answer the bell, to claw back, to make the kind of adjustments that define a playoff team. You can’t blame the hitters for not delivering in the clutch, but you can’t ignore the fact that the pitching staff, for all its flashes of brilliance, keeps folding under pressure.  

The bullpen, that fragile ecosystem of hope and desperation, has become a liability. You’d think with the talent in the pen—Gray, Scherzer, the occasional spark from a reliever named “Drew” or “Molina”—you’d have a chance to hold leads. But here we are, 8-7, 1.0 games behind the Cubs in the NL Central, with the kind of

optimist.name: Gretchen Hartmann
optimist.column:
The Cardinals lost yesterday, but let’s not dwell on that. Let’s talk about the magic of Busch Stadium, the kind that turns September into a fairy tale. You know, the kind that made a grandma from Kirkwood scream “Devil magic, baby” in the middle of Game 6 in 2011. That’s the kind of magic that’s still in the air, even if the scoreboard says otherwise.  

Eight games, seven losses. A 1.0-game gap in the NL Central. Sounds dire, but let’s not forget: the Cardinals are a team that’s built for comebacks. They’ve been here before. In 2011, they were down 3-0 in the World Series and came back to win it all. That’s not just luck—it’s the DNA of this franchise. You think an

pessimist.name: Vern Kratzinger
pessimist.column: The Cardinals lost again, to a team that’s not even in the same league. Boston didn’t just beat them—they embarrassed them, 9-3, in a game that felt like a dress rehearsal for a playoff sweep. You could argue the Red Sox are a better team, but that’s not the point.
